timeline.title: Project update
timeline.summary: ## Page 1 1 t-CyCIF protocol Day 0 Preparations ● Make sure that the tissue section being stained is not older than 6 months and has been stored at +40C. ● Set the oven’s temperature to 560C (double check the temperature using a thermometer). o Takes 30 minutes to preheat and oven in Kauppi’s lab has been more reliable ● Check the levels of xylene and alcohol in the dewaxing and rehydration series in the fume hood, if the levels are too low add more xylene or respective alcohol. Prepare fresh xy

protocols.title: t-CyCIF protocol
steps: Dewaxing and antigen retrieval (approx. 2h 10 min)
Boil for 4 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min.
Boil for 2 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min.
Boil for 2 min.
Let to cool down for 20 mins RT
Pre-staining (approx. 2h)
Bleaching/Fluorophore inactivation (approx. 2h)
Staining with secondaries and Hoechst (approx. 15 min)
De-coverslipping
Staining with primary antibodies
Staining with secondary antibodies
Cyclic phase – repeat steps as before

protocols.title: ## Sheet: Hoja1
steps: ## Sheet: Hoja1 Cycle Antibody Target Aids to identify Notes In stock PAN-CK Epithelial cells Tumor cells Works well yes E-Cadherin Tumor cells Works well yes CK-7 Tumor cells Chose only one to identify tumor cells more specifically.
CK-7 used by Färkkilä lab.
SOX2, S100A10 and EGFR shown to be overexpressed in FA tumors by Webster et.
al., 2022 yes SOX2 FA tumors S100A10 EGFR Vimentin Stroma Used by Färkkilä lab, worked poorly in our smaples yes CD31 High endothelial venules in TLSs Used by Joshi et al., 2015.

protocols.title: ## Sheet: INSTRUCTIONS
steps: ## Sheet: INSTRUCTIONS INSTRUCTIONS Make a copy of the template and run the functions from the top button tCycIF MagicGUI You should give permits for the automation (Google will ask you to authenticate just once) FOR RESEARCHERS 📋 Sample_Layout Panel refers to a specific combination of antibodies used in the full experiment (e.g., A, B, C...).
Versions within each panel indicate the sample split by dilution used (e.g., 1, 2…).
For real experiments (not validations): → Assign Panel A → Use Dilution 1 only.
📋 Panel_Builder Start by launching the Panel Design Wizard in the t-CyCIF MagicGui.
This links to the most recent version of the Antibody Database Use the dropdown or input an antibody ID to auto-fill details.
Formatting (e.g., colors) will apply a few seconds after selection.
You MUST fill the validation dillution column/s, for version 1 just fill down ValidationDillution1, for v2, ValidationDillution2 ...

If you are running a real experiment, not a validation,just fill ValidationDillution1 column, you should copy the ValidationDillutionLola for those antibodies that were validated ⚠️ IMPORTANT: If an antibody is validated but missing, update the antibody database, not just your local sheet.
After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version You MUST fill the cycle number and the panel.
If an antibody vial is missing you will be notified 📊 Panel_summary For presentations, publications, or organization, run 📊 Display Panel_summary in tCyCIF MagicGui.

This will display a new tab per each panel, with format, cycle number, marker number in the final image and all kinds of details This is useful for later computational steps FOR RESEARCHERS 🧪 Pipetting_Calculator You can adjust per-slide volume and Hoechst dilution for automatic calculations.

🧪 RunPipettingSteps If you go to the cycif gui and then runpipettingsteps you will get 🧪 TubePrepAuto Antibodies + exact volumes to pipet, organized by vial - set of slides 🧪 ReagentList List of required antibodies (calculates total volume needed + locations) → Useful for verifying inventory and ordering.

CyCIF image quantification and analysis (Cell type correlations)
We found M2 macrophage spatial arrangements at the epithelium that correlated with exhausted T cells
Wasserstein distances showed distinct differences between FA and non-FA tissue topology
TDA showed that FA tumors have a distinct topological organization compared to non-FA tumors
FA has a significant increase in neighborhoods where lymphoid cells interact with M2 macrophages, endothelial and stromal cells
with tumor progression (Fig. 4), particularly of exhausted T cells (Fig. 5). Macroscale analysis showed that FA tumors exclude immune
cells (Fig. 6), and neighborhood analysis identified that FA tumors exhaust T cells through a spatially restricted two-tier mechanism,
dendritic cells to areas of tumor formation (Fig. 8). Our working model suggests that in FA SCCs T cells interact with exhaustion -
stroma these T cells form RCNs with dendritic cells, suggesting an active two-tier exhaustion mechanism in these epithelial tumors .

Conclusion: We have characterized through spatial proteomics the TME of human and murine FA SCCs, as well as non-FA SCCs, and identified unique FA-specific topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression.
Result : We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased
diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression. In FA patients, there was
Conclusion : We profiled the TME of FA SCCs through image -based single -cell proteomics and identified
correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocal ization with immunosuppressive cell

## Page 1
1 
 t-CyCIF protocol 
Day 0 
Preparations 
● Make sure that the tissue section being stained is not older than 6 months and has been stored at 
+40C. 
 
● Set the oven’s temperature to 560C (double check the temperature using a thermometer). 
o Takes 30 minutes to preheat and oven in Kauppi’s lab has been more reliable 
 
● Check the levels of xylene and alcohol in the dewaxing and rehydration series in the fume hood, if the 
levels are too low add more xylene or respective alcohol. Prepare fresh xylene and alcohol series after 
using them 5 times. 
o Refer to sticky notes in the hood for dilution volumes 
o If helpful, record each time you use the xylene and alcohol series 
 
● Make sure that all the buffers and antibodies are available: antigen retrieval (100mM citric acid pH 
6.0), odyssey blocking buffer, 30% H202, & 480mM NaOH, and 1x PBS. 
● Preparing citric acid buffer, 10 mM, pH 6.0: 
o From 100mM stock 🡪 25mL of stock solution + 225mL of MQ. 
100 mM citric acid 1L: 
● Dissolve 21.0 grams of citric acid monohydrate (Sigma #C1909) to 800 mL of MQ 
H20. 
● Add 9.94 grams of NaOH pellets (Merck # 1310 -73-2) 
● Leave on a magnetic stirrer to thoroughly mix 
● Measure the pH and set to 6.0 
● Level the volume up to 1L with MQ H20. 
Note: Use the solution not more than 6 months after preparation 
 
● Preparing 10 L of 1x PBS 
o Empty remaining 1x PBS in glass jar by the paper towel dispenser 
o Add 1L of 10x PBS (aliquots located on the left counter by the window) 
o Head to supply room (B303) to collect MQ water with 10L jug 
o Review directions on the tank (double check that resistivit y is 18.2 MΩ) 
o Discard the first 200 mL of MQ before filling jug 
▪ Press button with a beaker symbol to view the volume discharged, adjust volume with

o collect MQ water with 10L jug 
o Review directions on the tank (double check that resistivit y is 18.2 MΩ) 
o Discard the first 200 mL of MQ before filling jug 
▪ Press button with a beaker symbol to view the volume discharged, adjust volume with 
up and down arrows 
o Press draw button above display screen 
o Shake jug to mix 
 


## Page 2
2 
 
Day 1 (approx. 6.5 h) 
1. Dewaxing and antigen retrieval (approx. 2h 10 min) 
1.1. Baking: place the slides vertically into a grey plastic slide holder and bake in a 560C oven for 
30 min. 
 
1.2. Deparaffinization + rehydration: 
3 x 5 min Xylene 
1 x 3 min 100% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 90% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 70% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 50% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 30% ethanol 
2 x 1 min successive 1x PBS solution 
 
1.3. Antigen retrieval: 
Transfer the slides onto a white plastic holder (looks like a strainer with a long handle) and in 
a glass dish - fill with 250 mL citric acid buffer. 
 
Boil sections in a microwave at 600 W as follows: 
1. Boil for 4 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min. 
2. Boil for 2 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min. 
3. Boil for 2 min. 
4. Let to cool down for 20 mins RT 
Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
2. Pre-staining (approx. 2h) 
2.1. Prepare a moist incubation chamber to minimize evaporation: Wet regular tissue paper 
under the tap water and fold it to fit inside the grey slide box . 
2.2. After cooling and washing from step 1. 3., take out the slides one at a time, dry the 
surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber -free tissue paper . 
2.3. Without letting the tissue dry, quickly add 200 -300 uL odyssey blocking buffer per section 
and incubate for 30 min RT. The use of PAP pen is not needed . Do not spread the blocking

of the tissue section using soft fiber -free tissue paper . 
2.3. Without letting the tissue dry, quickly add 200 -300 uL odyssey blocking buffer per section 
and incubate for 30 min RT. The use of PAP pen is not needed . Do not spread the blocking 
buffer with a pipette t ip, as you might scratch the tissue while doing so . In addition, thorough 
drying of the surroundings of the tissue creates a hydrophobic surface so that the solution 
does not spread outside the tissue. 
2.3.1. During incubation prepare for step 2.5. 
2.4. After the incu bation, remove the blocking buffer by vertically tapping the slide on a tissue 
paper. 
2.5. Next, pre-stain the slides with secondary antibodies and Hoechst only by adding 200 -300uL 
of pre -diluted antibodies per section. Incubate for 60 min at RT in a moist cha mber (from 
2.1). 
 
 Prepare cocktail of secondary antibodies and Hoechst in odyssey blocking buffer: 
 488 goat anti -rabbit 1:1000 (subtract amount of Ab from total vol: 1uL Ab: 999uL buffer) 
 555 goat anti -rat 1:1000 
 647 goat anti -mouse 1:1000 
 HOECHST 1:5000 
 Note: We add non -specific secondary antibodies to reduce autofluorescence 
 

## Page 3
3 
 2.6. Transfer the slides into a vertical grey plastic holder placed in a white plastic container with 
1xPBS. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
 
3. Bleaching/Fluorophore inactivation (approx. 2h) 
3.1. Prepare 50 mL bleaching solution in a falcon tube as follows: 
40 ml 1xPBS 
 7.5ml 30% H2O2 
 2.5ml 480 mM NaOH ( note: reaction begins when NaOH is added ) 
 
3.2. Pour the bleaching solution into a white slide box lid placed on top of a LED light. 
3.3. Using forceps, transfer the slides into a bleaching solution; make sure that the slides are 
comp letely immersed in the solution.

when NaOH is added ) 
 
3.2. Pour the bleaching solution into a white slide box lid placed on top of a LED light. 
3.3. Using forceps, transfer the slides into a bleaching solution; make sure that the slides are 
comp letely immersed in the solution. Place another LED light on top of the slide box (step 
3.2). Connect bottom and top LED lights to power. 
3.4. Photobleach the sides for 90 min at RT, during which change the buffer solution after every 
30 min for cycles 0 and 1, and for the rest of the cycles change the bleaching solution after 45 
min 
3.5. Wash four ti mes in PBS , ~ 3 min each. 
4. Staining with secondaries and Hoechst (approx. 15 min) 
4.1. During the washing step from 3.4., prepare a cocktail of secondary antibodies and Hoechst in 
odyssey blocking buffer 
488 goat anti -rabbit 1:2000 
 555 goat anti -rat 1:2000 
 647 goat anti -mouse 1:2000 
 HOECHST 1:10 000 
NOTE: Dilutions are different from step 2.2. And this round of secondary antibodies will be 
scanned and used as a negative control 
4.2. Add 200-300uL per section and incubate at +4°C o/n in a moist chamber (from 2.1.) 
Day 2 
4.3. Wash the slides three times in 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each 
4.4. Dry the surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber -free tissue paper. Add 100 uL of 
70% glycerol per section and carefully cover with a coverslip, avoiding air bubbles. 
4.5. If air bubbles appear, de -coverslip and try again 
4.6. Scan the slides. 
NOTE: Remove excess glycerol from the surrounding of the slides; the slide holder will 
 become dirty if the slide edges are moist and contain excess glycerol. 
 
5. De-coverslipping 
5.1. Immerse the slides at an angle into the plastic container with 1x PBS, so that the surface with

e surrounding of the slides; the slide holder will 
 become dirty if the slide edges are moist and contain excess glycerol. 
 
5. De-coverslipping 
5.1. Immerse the slides at an angle into the plastic container with 1x PBS, so that the surface with 
the coverslip is facing downward and the slide is orientated with the positive side submerged 
into the plastic container. 
5.2. Leave for about 5 min. Carefully lift the slide out of the cuvette and place into another grey 
slide holder in a white plastic container with 1x PBS. 

## Page 4
4 
 NOTE: when lifting the slide, the coverslip should automatically come off. Leave the slides in the 1x 
 PBS for another 5 min if this does not happen. Repeat with a new batch of PBS if needed. 
5.3. Wash three times with RT 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
6. Staining with primary antibodies 
6.1. Prepare wet chamber and place at +4 °C 
6.2. Stain with unconjugated primary antibodies o/n at +4 °C diluted in Odyssey solution. 
Day 3 
7. Staining with secondary antibodies 
7.1. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
7.2. Incubate secondary antibodies 1:2000 and Hoechst 1:10000 in Odyssey buffer for 1 hr RT

7.3. Wash three times with 1x PBS x3, ~ 3 min each. 
7.4. Dry the surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber-free tissue paper. Add 100 uL of 
70% glycerol per section and carefully cover with a coverslip, avoiding air bubbles. 
7.5. Scan the slides. 
NOTE: Volume of the 70% glycerol depends on the size of the tissue, bigger tissues may need more 
than 100ul for example 1cm x 1cm tissue might need 120 ul of glycerol

8. Cyclic phase – repeat steps as before 
8.1. Decoverslip using 1x PBS 
8.2. Photobleach for 1.5 h, change the solution after 45 min. 
8.3. Wash four times in PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
8.4. Stain with pre -conjugated antibodies and Hoechst 1:10000 o/n at +4°C. 
8.5. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
8.6. Mount with 70% glycerol and scan 
Repeat step 8 for the next round

## Sheet: INSTRUCTIONS
INSTRUCTIONS
Make a copy of the template and run the functions from the top button tCycIF MagicGUI
You should give permits for the automation (Google will ask you to authenticate just once)
FOR RESEARCHERS
📋 Sample_Layout
Panel refers to a specific combination of antibodies used in the full experiment (e.g., A, B, C...).
Versions within each panel indicate the sample split by dilution used (e.g., 1, 2…).
For real experiments (not validations): → Assign Panel A → Use Dilution 1 only.
📋 Panel_Builder
Start by launching the Panel Design Wizard in the t-CyCIF MagicGui.
This links to the most recent version of the Antibody Database
Use the dropdown or input an antibody ID to auto-fill details.
Formatting (e.g., colors) will apply a few seconds after selection.
You MUST fill the validation dillution column/s, for version 1 just fill down ValidationDillution1, for v2, ValidationDillution2 ...
If you are running a real experiment, not a validation,just fill ValidationDillution1 column, you should copy the ValidationDillutionLola for those antibodies that were validated
⚠️ IMPORTANT: If an antibody is validated but missing, update the antibody database, not just your local sheet. After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version
You MUST fill the cycle number and the panel.
If an antibody vial is missing you will be notified
📊 Panel_summary
For presentations, publications, or organization, run 📊 Display Panel_summary in tCyCIF MagicGui.
This will display a new tab per each panel, with format, cycle number, marker number in the final image and all kinds of details
This is useful for later computational steps
FOR RESEARCHERS
🧪 Pipetting_Calculator

ay Panel_summary in tCyCIF MagicGui.
This will display a new tab per each panel, with format, cycle number, marker number in the final image and all kinds of details
This is useful for later computational steps
FOR RESEARCHERS
🧪 Pipetting_Calculator
You can adjust per-slide volume and Hoechst dilution for automatic calculations.
🧪 RunPipettingSteps
If you go to the cycif gui and then runpipettingsteps you will get
🧪 TubePrepAuto Antibodies + exact volumes to pipet, organized by vial - set of slides
🧪 ReagentList List of required antibodies (calculates total volume needed + locations) → Useful for verifying inventory and ordering.
🧪 TubePrepAuto By default Slide_Number is displaye in Tube prep auto plan.. To change this setting to Unique_Section_ID, go to extensions->App scripts->tCycIF pipetting plan automation->generatePipettingPlan.gs in row 22 Slide_number to Unique_Section_ID.Remember to click the saving disk on top
🔬 RunScanningGUIs
🔬 Scanning_Plan It will display the plan for scanning with slideIDs, running numbers and dropdown menus that autoformat
🔬 Experimental_Assessment Automatically displays the sheet
FOR ANALYSES
💻 Display Computational Steps
Lists the samples and the steps needed in the future

#### Script to prepare single cell reference to be added to Xenium Web Panel Designer.

install.packages("BiocManager")

library(DropletUtils)
library(biomaRt)
library(Seurat)

sc_OC <- readRDS("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Results/escc_combined.rds")

#verify if values are integers or not (detect previous transformations)
all.equal(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x, as.integer(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x))
head(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x)

head(rownames(sc_OC))

dplyr::glimpse(GetAssay(sc_OC)@meta.data)

dplyr::glimpse(sc_OC[[]])


# Connect to Ensembl
mart <- useMart("ensembl", dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl")

# Get mapping
gene_map <- getBM(
 attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "hgnc_symbol"),
 filters = "hgnc_symbol",
 values = rownames(sc_OC),
 mart = mart
)

# Merge mapping back to your data
gene_map <- gene_map[gene_map$hgnc_symbol != "", ] # remove empty mappings


# Keep only genes with an Ensembl ID and present in the Seurat object
gene_map <- gene_map[gene_map$ensembl_gene_id != "" & 
 gene_map$hgnc_symbol %in% rownames(sc_OC), ]

# Remove duplicates (if any)
gene_map <- gene_map[!duplicated(gene_map$hgnc_symbol), ]

sc_OC <- subset(sc_OC, features = gene_map$hgnc_symbol)
dim(sc_OC)

# Store original gene symbols in meta.data
sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$hgnc_symbol <- (gene_map$hgnc_symbol)


# Ensure gene_map is in the same order as the Seurat object
gene_map <- gene_map[match(rownames(sc_OC), gene_map$hgnc_symbol), ]

# Add Ensembl IDs to meta.data in the RNA assay
sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$ensembl_id <- gene_map$ensembl_gene_id

head(sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data)

saveRDS(sc_OC, "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/xenium_scOC.rds")

# Run function

Add Ensembl IDs to meta.data in the RNA assay
sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$ensembl_id <- gene_map$ensembl_gene_id

head(sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data)

saveRDS(sc_OC, "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/xenium_scOC.rds")

# Run function
write10xCounts(
 "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/reference_data_xenium",
 GetAssayData(sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts"),
 gene.id = sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$ensembl_id ,
 gene.symbol = sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$hgnc_symbol ,
 barcodes = colnames(sc_OC),
 type = "sparse",
 version = "3"
)

# List the files
list.files("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/reference_data_xenium")

# Output
#[1] "barcodes.tsv.gz" "features.tsv.gz" "matrix.mtx.gz"


# Define function 
bundleOutputs <- function(out_dir, data, barcodes = colnames(data), cell_type = "annotated_type", subset = 1:length(barcodes)) {
 
 if (require("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {
 data.table::fwrite(
 data.table::data.table(
 barcode = barcodes,
 annotation = unlist(data[[cell_type]])
 )[subset, ],
 file.path(out_dir, "annotations.csv")
 )
 } else {
 write.table(
 data.frame(
 barcode = barcodes,
 annotation = unlist(data[[cell_type]])
 )[subset, ],
 file.path(out_dir, "annotations.csv"),
 sep = ",", row.names = FALSE
 )
 }
 
 bundle <- file.path(out_dir, paste0(basename(out_dir), ".zip"))
 
 utils::zip(
 bundle,
 list.files(out_dir, full.names = TRUE),
 zip = "zip"
 )
 
 if (file.info(bundle)$size / 1e6 > 2000) {
 warning("The output file is more than 2G and will need to be subset further.")
 }
}

bundleOutputs(out_dir = "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference", data = sc_OC)

list.files("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/")

## Cell 35 (code)
import pandas as pd

def compare_csv_files(file1, file2, output_file=None):
 """
 Compares two CSV files and detects differences at the cell level.
 
 Parameters:
 file1 (str): Path to the first CSV file.
 file2 (str): Path to the second CSV file.
 output_file (str, optional): Path to save the output differences as a CSV file.
 
 Returns:
 pd.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the differing cells.
 """
 # Load CSV files
 df1 = pd.read_csv(file1)
 df2 = pd.read_csv(file2)
 
 # Ensure both dataframes have the same shape
 if df1.shape != df2.shape:
 raise ValueError("The CSV files have different dimensions and cannot be compared directly.")
 
 # Identify differences
 diffs = df1 != df2

ile1)
 df2 = pd.read_csv(file2)
 
 # Ensure both dataframes have the same shape
 if df1.shape != df2.shape:
 raise ValueError("The CSV files have different dimensions and cannot be compared directly.")
 
 # Identify differences
 diffs = df1 != df2
 
 # Extract the differing values
 diff_indices = diffs.stack()[diffs.stack()].index.tolist()
 diff_values = []
 for index, column in diff_indices:
 row = {"Row Index": index, "Column": column, file1: df1.at[index, column], file2: df2.at[index, column]}
 diff_values.append(row)
 
 # Convert to DataFrame
 diff_df = pd.DataFrame(diff_values)
 
 # Save output if specified
 if output_file:
 diff_df.to_csv(output_file, index=False)
 
 return diff_df

# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
 file1 = "/media/cruz-osuna/Spatial/CycIF_human_2024/7_Analysis/Metadata/phenotype_calling/gatings/clean_csv/gating_1_cleaned.csv"
 file2 = "/media/cruz-osuna/Spatial/CycIF_human_2024/7_Analysis/Metadata/phenotype_calling/gatings/clean_csv/gating_2_cleaned.csv"
 output_file = "/media/cruz-osuna/Spatial/CycIF_human_2024/7_Analysis/Metadata/phenotype_calling/gatings/output_differences.csv"
 
 differences = compare_csv_files(file1, file2, output_file)
 print(differences)

 df.groupby([sample_col, rcn_col, group_col], observed=True)[marker_col]

 .reset_index(name="proportion")
)

## Cell 30 (code)
# Imports for clustering evaluation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score, davies_bouldin_score, calinski_harabasz_score

## Cell 31 (code)
# Extract spatial count data for ALL cells (not subset)
X = adata.uns["spatial_count_150"].copy()

print(f"✓ Extracted spatial_count_150 data")
print(f" Matrix shape: {X.shape}")
print(f" Total cells: {adata.n_obs}")

# Scale the data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Run PCA
pca = PCA()
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Explained variance
explained_variance = pca.explained_variance_ratio_ * 100
cumulative_variance = np.cumsum(explained_variance)

# Create two subplots side by side
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

# Plot 1: Individual variance explained per component
ax1.plot(range(1, len(explained_variance) + 1), explained_variance, marker='o', color='#31688e', linewidth=2)
ax1.set_xlabel("Number of Principal Components", fontsize=12)
ax1.set_ylabel("% of Variance Explained", fontsize=12)
ax1.set_title("Variance Explained per Component", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Plot 2: Cumulative variance explained
ax2.plot(range(1, len(cumulative_variance) + 1), cumulative_variance, marker='o', color='#31688e', linewidth=2)
ax2.axhline(70, color='#35b779', linestyle='--', label="70% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)
ax2.axhline(80, color='#fde724', linestyle='--', label="80% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)
ax2.axhline(90, color='#440154', linestyle='--', label="90% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)

e='--', label="70% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)
ax2.axhline(80, color='#fde724', linestyle='--', label="80% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)
ax2.axhline(90, color='#440154', linestyle='--', label="90% cumulative variance", linewidth=1.5)
ax2.set_xlabel("Number of Principal Components", fontsize=12)
ax2.set_ylabel("% of Cumulative Variance Explained", fontsize=12)
ax2.set_title("Cumulative Variance Explained", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax2.legend(fontsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(dir_out_plots, "PCA_variance_spatial_count_150.png"), dpi=1200, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

## Cell 32 (code)
# Variance table
df_variance = pd.DataFrame({
 "PC": [f"PC{i+1}" for i in range(len(explained_variance))],
 "Varianza (%)": explained_variance,
 "Varianza acumulada (%)": cumulative_variance
})

# Save table
df_variance.to_csv(os.path.join(dir_out_calculations, "pca_variance_explained_150.csv"), index=False)

display(df_variance.head(20))

## Cell 33 (code)
# Alternative criteria for selecting number of PCs

print("=" * 70)
print("ALTERNATIVE CRITERIA FOR PC SELECTION")
print("=" * 70)

# Criterion 1: Cumulative variance thresholds
thresholds = [70, 75, 80, 85, 90, 95]
print("\n Criterion 1: Cumulative variance thresholds")
for thresh in thresholds:
 n_pcs = np.where(cumulative_variance >= thresh)[0][0] + 1
 print(f" {thresh}% variance → Use {n_pcs} PCs")

# Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (eigenvalue > average)
# Count how many PCs have explained variance greater than the average variance.
avg_variance = 100 / len(explained_variance)

nce >= thresh)[0][0] + 1
 print(f" {thresh}% variance → Use {n_pcs} PCs")

# Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (eigenvalue > average)
# Count how many PCs have explained variance greater than the average variance.
avg_variance = 100 / len(explained_variance)
kaiser_pcs = np.sum(explained_variance > avg_variance)
print(f"\n Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (variance > {avg_variance:.2f}%)")
print(f" Use {kaiser_pcs} PCs")

# Criterion 3: Look for biggest drop in variance
variance_diff = np.diff(explained_variance)
biggest_drop_idx = np.argmin(variance_diff) + 1
print(f"\n Criterion 3: Biggest drop in variance")
print(f" Use {biggest_drop_idx} PCs (biggest drop after PC{biggest_drop_idx})")
print("\n" + "=" * 70)

# Save criteria results in csv file
criteria_results = {
 "Criterion": ["70% Variance", "75% Variance", "80% Variance", "85% Variance", "90% Variance", "95% Variance", "Kaiser Rule", "Biggest Drop"],
 "Number of PCs": []
}
for thresh in thresholds:
 n_pcs = np.where(cumulative_variance >= thresh)[0][0] + 1
 criteria_results["Number of PCs"].append(n_pcs)
criteria_results["Number of PCs"].append(kaiser_pcs)
criteria_results["Number of PCs"].append(biggest_drop_idx)
criteria_df = pd.DataFrame(criteria_results)
criteria_df.to_csv(os.path.join(dir_out_calculations, "pca_pc_selection_criteria_150.csv"), index=False)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score, davies_bouldin_score, calinski_harabasz_score
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import os


## Cell 39 (code)
# K-MEANS CLUSTERING: ELBOW METHOD AND OPTIMAL K DETERMINATION
# =====================================================================

# Use the same PCA-transformed data and number of PCs as before
n_pcs_to_use = 17 
X_pca_subset = X_pca[:, :n_pcs_to_use]

# Define range of k values to test
k_range = range(2, 31) # Test from 2 to 30 clusters

# Initialize lists to store metrics
inertias = []
silhouette_scores = []
calinski_scores = []
davies_bouldin_scores = []

print("=" * 70)
print("TESTING K-MEANS WITH DIFFERENT K VALUES")
print("=" * 70)

# Compute metrics for each k
for k in k_range:
 # Fit K-means
 kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
 labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_pca_subset)
 
 # Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares)
 inertias.append(kmeans.inertia_)
 
 # Silhouette Score (higher is better)
 sil_score = silhouette_score(X_pca_subset, labels, metric='euclidean', sample_size=10000)
 silhouette_scores.append(sil_score)
 
 # Calinski-Harabasz Index (higher is better)
 ch_score = calinski_harabasz_score(X_pca_subset, labels)
 calinski_scores.append(ch_score)
 
 # Davies-Bouldin Index (lower is better)
 db_score = davies_bouldin_score(X_pca_subset, labels)
 davies_bouldin_scores.append(db_score)

 print(f"K={k:2d}: Inertia={kmeans.inertia_:.1f} | Silhouette={sil_score:.3f} | CH={ch_score:.1f} | DB={db_score:.3f}")

print("=" * 70)

# Create comprehensive visualization

# =====================================================================
# DETERMINE OPTIMAL K
# =====================================================================

# Method 1: Elbow method using rate of decrease in inertia
inertia_diff = np.diff(inertias)
inertia_diff2 = np.diff(inertia_diff) # Second derivative
elbow_k = np.argmax(inertia_diff2) + 3 # +3 because: +2 for k_range starting at 2, +1 for double diff

# Method 2: Best Silhouette Score
best_sil_k = list(k_range)[np.argmax(silhouette_scores)]

# Method 3: Best Calinski-Harabasz
best_ch_k = list(k_range)[np.argmax(calinski_scores)]

# Method 4: Best Davies-Bouldin (minimum)
best_db_k = list(k_range)[np.argmin(davies_bouldin_scores)]

# Normalize metrics for composite score
norm_sil = (np.array(silhouette_scores) + 1) / 2

sz

# Method 4: Best Davies-Bouldin (minimum)
best_db_k = list(k_range)[np.argmin(davies_bouldin_scores)]

# Normalize metrics for composite score
norm_sil = (np.array(silhouette_scores) + 1) / 2
norm_ch = np.array(calinski_scores) / max(calinski_scores)
norm_db = 1 - (np.array(davies_bouldin_scores) / (max(davies_bouldin_scores) * 2))
composite_score_kmeans = (norm_sil + norm_ch + norm_db) / 3
best_comp_k = list(k_range)[np.argmax(composite_score_kmeans)]

# Create summary DataFrame
summary_kmeans = pd.DataFrame({
 'K': list(k_range),
 'Inertia': inertias,
 'Silhouette_Score': silhouette_scores,
 'Calinski_Harabasz': calinski_scores,
 'Davies_Bouldin': davies_bouldin_scores,
 'Composite_Score': composite_score_kmeans
})

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("RECOMMENDED K VALUES FOR K-MEANS")
print("=" * 70)

print(f"\n Method 1: Elbow Method (2nd derivative)")

print(f" Recommended K: {best_sil_k}")
print(f" Score: {summary_kmeans.loc[summary_kmeans['K']==best_sil_k, 'Silhouette_Score'].values[0]:.3f}")

print(f"\n Method 4: Best Davies-Bouldin (minimum)")

print(f" Composite: {summary_kmeans.loc[summary_kmeans['K']==best_comp_k, 'Composite_Score'].values[0]:.3f}")

## Cell 50 (code)
# Statistical enrichment analysis: Chi-square test
# Test if phenotype distributions differ significantly across clusters

from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

# Create contingency table (raw counts, not percentages)
contingency = pd.crosstab(
 adata.obs[cluster_col], 
 adata.obs['FinalPhenotype']
)

# Perform chi-square test
chi2, p_value, dof, expected_freq = chi2_contingency(contingency)

print("=" * 70)
print("STATISTICAL ENRICHMENT ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"\nChi-square test for phenotype distribution across clusters:")
print(f" Chi-square statistic: {chi2:.2f}")
print(f" P-value: {p_value:.2e}")
print(f" Degrees of freedom: {dof}")

if p_value < 0.001:
 print(f"\n✓ Result: HIGHLY SIGNIFICANT (p < 0.001)")
 print(" Cell phenotypes are NOT randomly distributed across clusters.")
 print(" Each cluster has a distinct cellular composition.")
else:
 print(f"\n Result: Not significant (p = {p_value:.4f})")

# Calculate enrichment scores (observed vs expected)
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("ENRICHMENT ANALYSIS (Observed/Expected ratio)")
print("=" * 70)

# Convert expected frequencies back to DataFrame
expected_df = pd.DataFrame(
 expected_freq, 
 index=contingency.index, 
 columns=contingency.columns
)

# Calculate enrichment ratio
enrichment = contingency / expected_df

# Show top enrichments per cluster
print("\nTop 3 enriched phenotypes per cluster (Obs/Exp ratio):")
for cluster in sorted(enrichment.index):
 top_enriched = enrichment.loc[cluster].nlargest(3)
 print(f"\nCluster {cluster}:")
 for pheno, ratio in top_enriched.items():
 obs = contingency.loc[cluster, pheno]
 print(f" • {pheno}: {ratio:.2f}x (n={obs} cells)")

r in sorted(enrichment.index):
 top_enriched = enrichment.loc[cluster].nlargest(3)
 print(f"\nCluster {cluster}:")
 for pheno, ratio in top_enriched.items():
 obs = contingency.loc[cluster, pheno]
 print(f" • {pheno}: {ratio:.2f}x (n={obs} cells)")

# Save enrichment table
enrichment.to_csv(os.path.join(dir_out_calculations, "cluster_phenotype_enrichment.csv"))
print(f"\n✓ Enrichment table saved to: {dir_out_calculations}")

def make_bin_label(dist, prefix):
 return (
 prefix + "_" +
 ((dist // 150) * 150).astype("Int64").astype(str) +
 "–" +
 (((dist // 150) * 150) + 150).astype("Int64").astype(str) +
 "µm"
 )

# 3️⃣ Crear tissue_section combinando ambas

 adata.obs.loc[mask_stroma, "stroma_dist_um"],
 "stroma"
)

# epithelium donde stroma es NA
mask_epi = adata.obs["stroma_dist_um"].isna() & adata.obs["epithelium_dist_um"].notna()
adata.obs.loc[mask_epi, "tissue_section"] = make_bin_label(
 adata.obs.loc[mask_epi, "epithelium_dist_um"],

)

## Cell 67 (code)
# =====================================================================
# ALTERNATIVE CRITERIA FOR PC SELECTION (CLUSTER CHARACTERIZATION)
# =====================================================================

print("=" * 70)
print("ALTERNATIVE CRITERIA FOR PC SELECTION")
print("=" * 70)

# Use the variance from cluster characterization PCA
# Criterion 1: Cumulative variance thresholds
thresholds = [70, 75, 80, 85, 90, 95]
print("\n Criterion 1: Cumulative variance thresholds")
threshold_pcs = []
for thresh in thresholds:
 matching_pcs = np.where(cumulative_variance >= thresh)[0]
 if len(matching_pcs) > 0:
 n_pcs = matching_pcs[0] + 1
 threshold_pcs.append(n_pcs)
 print(f" {thresh}% variance → Use {n_pcs} PCs")
 else:
 threshold_pcs.append(len(explained_variance))
 print(f" {thresh}% variance → Use all {len(explained_variance)} PCs (threshold not reached)")

# Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (eigenvalue > average)
# Count how many PCs have explained variance greater than the average variance.
avg_variance = 100 / len(explained_variance)
kaiser_pcs = np.sum(explained_variance > avg_variance)
print(f"\n Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (variance > {avg_variance:.2f}%)")
print(f" Use {kaiser_pcs} PCs")

d variance greater than the average variance.
avg_variance = 100 / len(explained_variance)
kaiser_pcs = np.sum(explained_variance > avg_variance)
print(f"\n Criterion 2: Kaiser rule (variance > {avg_variance:.2f}%)")
print(f" Use {kaiser_pcs} PCs")

# Criterion 3: Look for biggest drop in variance
variance_diff = np.diff(explained_variance)
biggest_drop_idx = np.argmin(variance_diff) + 1
print(f"\n Criterion 3: Biggest drop in variance")
print(f" Use {biggest_drop_idx} PCs (biggest drop after PC{biggest_drop_idx})")

# Criterion 4: Elbow method (optional - based on rate of change)
# Find where the second derivative changes most
if len(explained_variance) > 2:
 second_diff = np.diff(variance_diff)
 elbow_idx = np.argmax(second_diff) + 2 # +2 because of double diff
 print(f"\n Criterion 4: Elbow method (2nd derivative)")
 print(f" Use {elbow_idx} PCs")
else:
 elbow_idx = 2

print("=" * 70)

# Calculate pairwise distances in PCA space using all PCs
distances = pdist(X_pca, metric='euclidean')
distance_matrix = squareform(distances)
distance_df = pd.DataFrame(
 distance_matrix,
 index=characterization_matrix.index,
 columns=characterization_matrix.index
)

# Find pairs of similar clusters (distance < threshold)
threshold = np.percentile(distances, 25) # Bottom 25% distances
print(f"\nDistance threshold for similarity: {threshold:.2f}")

similar_pairs = []
for i in range(len(distance_df)):
 for j in range(i+1, len(distance_df)):
 if distance_matrix[i, j] < threshold:
 similar_pairs.append({
 'Cluster_A': distance_df.index[i],
 'Cluster_B': distance_df.index[j],
 'Distance': distance_matrix[i, j]
 })

similar_pairs_df = pd.DataFrame(similar_pairs).sort_values('Distance')

print(f"\nFound {len(similar_pairs_df)} pairs of similar clusters:")

luster_A': distance_df.index[i],
 'Cluster_B': distance_df.index[j],
 'Distance': distance_matrix[i, j]
 })

similar_pairs_df = pd.DataFrame(similar_pairs).sort_values('Distance')

print(f"\nFound {len(similar_pairs_df)} pairs of similar clusters:")
display(similar_pairs_df)

# Hierarchical clustering using all PCs
linkage_matrix = linkage(X_pca, method='ward')

# Dendrogram
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
dendrogram(
 linkage_matrix,
 labels=characterization_matrix.index.astype(str),
 leaf_font_size=12,
 color_threshold=threshold
)
plt.xlabel('Cluster', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.ylabel('Distance', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.title('Hierarchical Clustering of RCNs/Clusters', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.axhline(y=threshold, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label=f'Similarity threshold')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

distance_df.to_csv(os.path.join(dir_out_calculations, "cluster_distance_matrix_" + feature + ".csv"))

import pandas as pd

def compare_csv_files(file1, file2, output_file=None):
 """
 Compares two CSV files and detects differences at the cell level.
 
 Parameters:
 file1 (str): Path to the first CSV file.
 file2 (str): Path to the second CSV file.
 output_file (str, optional): Path to save the output differences as a CSV file.
 
 Returns:
 pd.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the differing cells.
 """
 # Load CSV files
 df1 = pd.read_csv(file1)
 df2 = pd.read_csv(file2)
 
 # Ensure both dataframes have the same shape
 if df1.shape != df2.shape:
 raise ValueError("The CSV files have different dimensions and cannot be compared directly.")
 
 # Identify differences
 diffs = df1 != df2

ile1)
 df2 = pd.read_csv(file2)
 
 # Ensure both dataframes have the same shape
 if df1.shape != df2.shape:
 raise ValueError("The CSV files have different dimensions and cannot be compared directly.")
 
 # Identify differences
 diffs = df1 != df2
 
 # Extract the differing values
 diff_indices = diffs.stack()[diffs.stack()].index.tolist()
 diff_values = []
 for index, column in diff_indices:
 row = {"Row Index": index, "Column": column, file1: df1.at[index, column], file2: df2.at[index, column]}
 diff_values.append(row)
 
 # Convert to DataFrame
 diff_df = pd.DataFrame(diff_values)
 
 # Save output if specified
 if output_file:
 diff_df.to_csv(output_file, index=False)
 
 return diff_df

## Cell 5 (code)
def compute_min_distance(
 adata,
 source_col="tissue_region",
 source_label="stroma",
 target_col="epithelium_nearest_to_stroma",
 target_label="nearest_neighbor",
 new_col = "distance_to_nearest_epithelium",
 sample_col="imageid",
 x_col= "X_centroid",
 y_col= "Y_centroid",
 conversion_factor = 0.325
):
 """
 Calculate minimum distance between source and target cells
 """
 # initialize column with NaN
 adata.obs[new_col] = np.nan

 for s in adata.obs[sample_col].unique():
 # sample subset
 sub = adata.obs[adata.obs[sample_col] == s]

 # source and target cells within sample
 source_cells = sub[sub[source_col] == source_label]
 target_cells = sub[sub[target_col] == target_label]

 # skip sample if no target cells
 if len(target_cells) == 0:
 continue

 # coordinates
 source_coords = source_cells[[x_col, y_col]].values
 target_coords = target_cells[[x_col, y_col]].values

 # KDTree per sample
 tree = KDTree(target_coords)
 distances, _ = tree.query(source_coords, k=1)

 # assign result ONLY to source cells in this sample
 adata.obs.loc[source_cells.index, new_col] = distances
 
 print(f"✔ Distances saved in adata.obs['{new_col}'] per sample.")
 
 adata.obs[new_col] = adata.obs[new_col]*conversion_factor

## Cell 6 (code)
def bin_by_distance(
 adata,
 um_col="distance_to_nearest_epithelium",
 new_col="stroma_by_distance",
 cell_type = "epithelium",
 max_um=1000,
 step=100
):
 """
 Bin cells into distance categories using micrometer distances
 """
 bins = np.arange(0, max_um + step, step)
 labels = [f"{cell_type}_{b}µm" for b in bins[1:]]

 adata.obs[new_col] = pd.cut(
 adata.obs[um_col],
 bins=bins,
 labels=labels,
 include_lowest=True,
 right=False
 )

 print(f"Column '{new_col}' created with bins of {step} µm up to {max_um} µm.")

def most_common(series):
 s = series.dropna()
 return s.mode().iloc[0] if not s.empty else None

# Identificar si cada fila pertenece a stroma o epithelium
tda_str = df["TDA"].astype(str).str.lower()
is_stroma = tda_str.str.contains("stroma")

# Seleccionar la columna correcta según stroma/epithelium
df["distance_col"] = df["stroma_by_distance"]
df.loc[~is_stroma, "distance_col"] = df["epithelium_by_distance"]

# Calcular la moda por grupo de TDA de manera vectorizada
df["TDA_by_distance"] = (
 df.groupby("TDA")["distance_col"]
 .transform(lambda s: most_common(s))
)

# Limpiar columna auxiliar
df.drop(columns=["distance_col"], inplace=True)

## Cell 8 (code)


def filter_cells_by_density(
 adata,
 filter_cell_type,
 min_cells,
 radius_um,
 label_col="tissue_region",
 x_col="X_centroid", 
 y_col="Y_centroid",
 imageid_col="imageid",
 conversion_factor=0.325, # micrometers per pixel
 density_filter_col="density_filter"
):
 
 """
 Filter cells based on local cell density and mark which ones pass the filter
 
 Parameters:
 - filter_cell_type: cell type to apply density filter to
 - min_cells: minimum number of same-type cells within radius
 - radius_um: search radius in micrometers
 - density_filter_col: new column that marks cells that pass density filter
 """
 
 """
 Optimized version: avoids loops, uses boolean masks, 
 minimizes pandas overhead and uses vectorized KDTree radius queries.
 """

 # Convert radius from micrometers to pixels
 radius_px = radius_um / conversion_factor

 # Create new column (False for all)
 adata.obs[density_filter_col] = False

 # Direct access for speed
 obs = adata.obs

ectorized KDTree radius queries.
 """

 # Convert radius from micrometers to pixels
 radius_px = radius_um / conversion_factor

 # Create new column (False for all)
 adata.obs[density_filter_col] = False

 # Direct access for speed
 obs = adata.obs

 total_cells = np.sum(obs[label_col] == filter_cell_type)
 filtered_count = 0

 # Loop only through images
 for img in obs[imageid_col].unique():

 mask_img = obs[imageid_col] == img
 sub = obs[mask_img]

 # mask for target cell type
 mask_target = sub[label_col] == filter_cell_type
 target_cells = sub[mask_target]

 if len(target_cells) < min_cells:
 continue

 coords = target_cells[[x_col, y_col]].to_numpy()

 # KDTree built once per image
 tree = KDTree(coords)

 # Query all at once (vectorized)
 neighbors_list = tree.query_radius(coords, r=radius_px)

 # Vector of which cells pass the filter
 pass_mask = np.array([len(n) >= min_cells for n in neighbors_list])

 # Update adata.obs efficiently
 passing_indices = target_cells.index[pass_mask]
 adata.obs.loc[passing_indices, density_filter_col] = True

 filtered_count += np.sum(pass_mask)

 # Summary
 print(f"✔ Density filter applied to '{filter_cell_type}' cells")
 print(f" - {filtered_count}/{total_cells} cells pass filter")
 print(f" - Criteria: ≥{min_cells} cells within {radius_um} µm radius")

 return adata

## Cell 15 (markdown)
#### 5) Pipeline execution function and setting of some parameters

## Cell 16 (code)
def compute_distance_pipeline(
 adata,
 source_type, # example: "epithelium"
 target_type, # example: "stroma"
 density_filter_min=4,
 density_filter_radius=50,
 pixel_size=0.325,
 max_um=1000,
 step_um=200,
 region_col="tissue_region",
 sample_col="imageid"
):
 """
 Executes the full pipeline: density filtering, nearest-neighbor assignment,
 distance computation, conversion to micrometers, and distance binning.
 """

 # ---- Step 1: Density filtering ----
 adata = filter_cells_by_density(
 adata,
 filter_cell_type=target_type,
 min_cells=density_filter_min,
 radius_um=density_filter_radius,
 )

 # ---- Step 2: Nearest neighbor ----
 nn_col = f"{source_type}_nearest_{target_type}"
 adata = nearest_neighbor_with_density_filter(
 adata,
 cell_1=source_type,
 cell_2=target_type,
 label_col=region_col
 )

 # ---- Step 3: Distance calculation ----
 distance_col = f"distance_{source_type}_to_{target_type}"
 compute_min_distance(
 adata,
 source_col=region_col,
 source_label=source_type,
 target_col=nn_col,
 target_label="nearest_neighbor",
 sample_col=sample_col,
 new_col=distance_col
 )

 # Convert distance to µm
 adata.obs[distance_col] = adata.obs[distance_col] * pixel_size

 # ---- Step 4: Binning ----
 bin_col = f"{source_type}_by_distance_to_{target_type}"
 bin_stroma_by_distance(
 adata,
 um_col=distance_col,
 new_col=bin_col,
 max_um=max_um,
 step=step_um
 )

 return adata

def filter_cells_by_density(
 adata,
 filter_cell_type, # cell type to filter (e.g., "epithelium")
 min_cells, # minimum number of cells required in radius
 radius_um, # radius in micrometers to search for neighbors
 label_col="tissue_region",
 x_col="X_centroid", 
 y_col="Y_centroid",
 imageid_col="imageid",
 conversion_factor=0.65, # pixel to micrometer conversion
 density_filter_col="density_filter" # new column to mark filtered cells
):
 """
 Filter cells based on local cell density and mark which ones pass the filter
 
 Parameters:
 - filter_cell_type: cell type to apply density filter to
 - min_cells: minimum number of same-type cells within radius
 - radius_um: search radius in micrometers
 - density_filter_col: new column that marks cells that pass density filter
 """
 
 # Convert radius from micrometers to pixels
 radius_px = radius_um / conversion_factor
 
 # Initialize density filter column
 adata.obs[density_filter_col] = False
 
 df = adata.obs.copy()
 
 for img in df[imageid_col].unique():
 sub = df[df[imageid_col] == img]
 
 # Cells of the specified type in this image
 target_cells = sub[sub[label_col] == filter_cell_type]

column
 adata.obs[density_filter_col] = False
 
 df = adata.obs.copy()
 
 for img in df[imageid_col].unique():
 sub = df[df[imageid_col] == img]
 
 # Cells of the specified type in this image
 target_cells = sub[sub[label_col] == filter_cell_type]
 
 if len(target_cells) < 2: # Need at least 2 cells for density calculation
 continue
 
 # Coordinates of target cells
 target_coords = target_cells[[x_col, y_col]].to_numpy()
 
 # KDTree for efficient neighbor search
 tree = KDTree(target_coords)
 
 cells_that_pass = []
 
 for idx, cell in target_cells.iterrows():
 # Current cell coordinates
 cell_coord = cell[[x_col, y_col]].values.reshape(1, -1)
 
 # Find cells of same type within radius (includes the cell itself)
 indices = tree.query_radius(cell_coord, r=radius_px)[0]
 
 # Count cells in radius (includes current cell)
 cells_in_radius = len(indices)
 
 # Mark cell as passing filter if density criteria is met
 if cells_in_radius >= min_cells:
 cells_that_pass.append(idx)
 
 # Update density filter for cells that pass in this image
 if cells_that_pass:
 adata.obs.loc[cells_that_pass, density_filter_col] = True
 
 total_cells = len(adata.obs[adata.obs[label_col] == filter_cell_type])
 filtered_cells = adata.obs[density_filter_col].sum()
 
 print(f"✔ Density filter applied to {filter_cell_type} cells")
 print(f" - {filtered_cells}/{total_cells} cells pass filter")
 print(f" - Criteria: minimum {min_cells} cells within {radius_um}µm radius")
 
 return adata

import math
import tifffile
import os
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
#INPUT_PATH = "./images"
#OUTPUT_PATH = "./output/" # remember to add / in the end

INPUT_PATH = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/02_Visualization/t-CycIF/Images_IC'
OUTPUT_PATH = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/08_Results/Datasets/09_ML/single_channel_images/'
channels_path = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/01_Registration/channels.txt'

channels = ["Hoechst_1"]

arr = os.listdir(INPUT_PATH)

if __name__ == '__main__':
 print("Image list: {}".format(arr)) # verify you only have the desired images here

 user_input = input("Do you want to proceed? [y/n]")
 if user_input=="y":
 for image in arr:
 if image.startswith('.'):
 print(f"Skipping hidden file: {image}")
 continue
 image_name = image
 sep = '.'
 imageid = image_name.split(sep, 1)[0]
 print("Imageid = {}".format(imageid))
 IMAGE_PATH = os.path.join(INPUT_PATH, image_name)
 print("reading image {}".format(IMAGE_PATH))

 with open(channels_path, 'r') as channel_txt:
 channel_list = [channel.rstrip() for channel in channel_txt]
 if channel_list[0] == "ABS":
 del channel_list[0]
 indexes = []
 for ch in channels: 
 index = channel_list.index(ch)
 indexes.append(index)

 merge = []
 for idx in indexes:
 img = tifffile.imread(IMAGE_PATH, key = idx)
 merge.append(img)
 
 stack = np.stack(merge, axis=0)

 output_name = OUTPUT_PATH + imageid + ".ome.tif"

 with tifffile.TiffWriter(output_name, bigtiff = True) as tif:
 for img_array in merge:
 tif.write(stack,
 photometric="minisblack",
 metadata={"axes": "CYX"})

 print("Image saved at {}".format(imageid))

## Slide 19
Take home conclusions
FA tumors show compartmentalized expression of exhaustion and functional cell markers compared to non FA tumors
M2 macrophages infiltrate preferentially the epithelium of the FA tumor, while dendritic cells the adjacent stroma, driving a two tier exhaustion process
TDA showed that FA tumors have a distinct topological organization compared to non-FA tumors

Title: Comparative analysis of scRNAseq data from patients with different inherited bone marrow failure syndromes 
Pablo Siliceo, Ángel A. de la Cruz, Ulises Juárez, Paula Leal-Anaya, Sara Frías, Alfredo Rodríguez
Introduction: Inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (IBMFS) are a set of heterogeneous genetic diseases caused by germline pathogenic variants in genes involved in hematopoiesis and genomic integrity. Despite sharing common phenotypical manifestations that have aided in opportune diagnosis, such as bone marrow failure, congenital malformations and an increased predisposition to develop cancer, little is known about the shared and distinctive transcriptional programs in their hematopoietic compartment that underlie the clinical manifestations of each disease. Proper patient stratification and characterization of transcriptional regulation behind bone marrow failure development and neoplastic transformation are crucial steps towards dissecting early mechanisms of disease progression and guidance of best treatment regimes. 
Objective: Identify transcriptional contingency and perform comparative functional characterization of scRNAseq datasets of hematopoietic stem progenitor cells (HSPC) of patients with IBMFS 
Methods: We integrated and analyzed three different publicly available scRNAseq datasets (10x Genomics) of lineage negative, CD34+ cells of bone marrow samples from patients with Fanconi Anemia (FA), Dyskeratosis Congenita (DC) and Diamond-Blackfan Anemia (DBA), as well as healthy controls.

different publicly available scRNAseq datasets (10x Genomics) of lineage negative, CD34+ cells of bone marrow samples from patients with Fanconi Anemia (FA), Dyskeratosis Congenita (DC) and Diamond-Blackfan Anemia (DBA), as well as healthy controls. By using R package Seurat, we integrated all datasets and performed standard quality control metrics (doublet removal, trimming of cells with high mitochondrial gene percentage and low gene detection and RNA counts) as well as batch effect correction using different methods (Harmony, scANVI, scVI, Seurat and Scanorama). Once integrated, we performed dimensionality reduction (PCA, UMAP), cell clustering and cell type annotation on the combined dataset to dissect the heterogeneity of the data and perform downstream analyses such as differential gene expression (DGE) and gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) through a comparative scheme between patients, syndromes, and healthy controls.
Results: We successfully integrated publicly available datasets of three different sample cohorts from patients with IBMFS: FA, DC and DBA. Through dimensionality reduction, cell clustering and celltype annotation we found distinctive clusters with shared celltypes between syndromes as well as populations with unique transcriptional programs who differed from patient to patient. By means of GSEA and DGE, we found shared enrichment and differential downregulation of genes and pathways related to DNA damage response, metabolic stress and, in some cell clusters, oncogenesis. 
Conclusions: Our work provides a basis for an accumulative, comparative and integrated atlas of scRNAseq data from patients with IBMFS. Our results show the integration of datasets from FA, DC and DBA, three major IBMFS.

cell clusters, oncogenesis. 
Conclusions: Our work provides a basis for an accumulative, comparative and integrated atlas of scRNAseq data from patients with IBMFS. Our results show the integration of datasets from FA, DC and DBA, three major IBMFS. Functional analyses of the combined dataset enabled us to find shared transcriptional programs of cellular stress and oncogenesis between syndromes, and between the celltypes therein contained.

Alfredo Rodríguez1;Pablo Siliceo2;Ada Junquera3;Luis A.Flores2;Mahiru Trejo2;Fernando Pérez -Villatoro3;Anna Laury3;Renee Fisher4;Gloria Sicilia5;

1)National Autonomous University ofMexico (UNAM), Mexico City ,Mexico ;2)National Autonomous University ofMexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico ;3)Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland ;4)University of
Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, United States ;5)National Institute ofPediatrics, Mexico City, Mexico ;6)National Autonomous University ofMexico (UNAM)/National Institute ofPediatrics, Mexico City, Mexico ;7)University of
Arkansas, Fayetteville, United States ;8)Universitätsklinikum Düsseldorf, Düsseldorf, Germany .
INTRODUCTION Clinical manifestations of Fanconi anemia (FA) include bone marrow failure and an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) 
at an early age. Their underlying DNA repair deficiency makes patients with FA hypersensitive to conventional DNA -damage -based cancer treatments, stressing the need for 
alternative therapeutic approaches . The implementation of single -cell spatial technologies in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) has shown promising insights 
towards the development of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses .
OBJETIVE . To identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using

ent of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses .
OBJETIVE . To identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using 
single -cell spatial proteomics .Contact: alfredo.rodriguez@iibiomedicas.unam. mx
RESULTS
CONCLUSIONS . We are characterizing the TME of SCCs from patients with FA using multidimensional imaging with single -cell spatial 
resolution (Fig. 1 and 2). We observe overall presence of tumor exhaustion markers (Fig. 3), as well as increased immune infiltration 
with tumor progression (Fig. 4), particularly of exhausted T cells (Fig. 5). Macroscale analysis showed that FA tumors exclude immune 
cells (Fig. 6), and neighborhood analysis identified that FA tumors exhaust T cells through a spatially restricted two-tier mechanism, 
mediated by M2 macrophages in the epithelium and dendritic cells in the adjacent stroma (Fig. 7). In our mouse model of FA 
carcinogenesis we found increasing numbers of γH2AX+ cells with tumor progression, and arrival of M2 macrophages followed by 
dendritic cells to areas of tumor formation (Fig. 8). Our working model suggests that in FA SCCs T cells interact with exhaustion -
promoting myeloid cells in two compartments, in the malignant epithelium T cells form RCNs with M2 macrophages and in the adjacent 
stroma these T cells form RCNs with dendritic cells, suggesting an active two-tier exhaustion mechanism in these epithelial tumors .

Title: Spatial landscape of Fanconi anemia tumors shows increasing immune exhaustion with carcinogenic progression.
Topic: Cancer
Authors: Pablo Siliceo Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera Mencia3, Fernando Pérez Villatoro3, Angela Szabo3, Ashwini Nagaraj3, Luis A. Flores Mejía1,2, Anna Laury3, Eunike Velleuer4, Anniina Färkkilä5,6, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2
1) Laboratorio de Falla Medular y Carcinogénesis, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 2) Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México. 3) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4) Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld. Germany. 5) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 6) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland.
Introduction. Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by bone marrow failure and an increased cancer predisposition. Advances in bone marrow transplantation (BMT) have reduced the aplastic anemia-related FA mortality; however, there has been an epidemiological shift on the FA morbi-mortality, where patients at a very early age are developing aggressive squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). New methodological avenues in the study of cancer, like the implementation of spatial proteomics with single-cell resolution using formalin-fixed and paraffin-embedded samples (FFPEs) of solid tumors, are paving the way for exciting new therapeutic insights targeting the tumor/immune landscape. 
Objective.

he implementation of spatial proteomics with single-cell resolution using formalin-fixed and paraffin-embedded samples (FFPEs) of solid tumors, are paving the way for exciting new therapeutic insights targeting the tumor/immune landscape. 
Objective. To study the roleplay of the tumor-immune interactions in FA carcinogenic progression by implementing single-cell spatial proteomics in archived FFPE SCC tumor samples from patients with FA.
Material and methods. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors using highly multiplexed tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) in 11 SCC FFPE tumor samples from patients with FA, with and without previous BMT. A certified pathologist annotated H&E slides of the specimens to identify the carcinogenic progression of the tumors in low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and invasive carcinoma. Slides were prepared from FFPE blocks that were deparaffinized, dehydrated and rehydrated in citrate buffer. Multiple staining cycles with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies were imaged in a Nikon Ni-E scanner. The resulting whole-slide multidimensional images were analyzed in the context of protein expression, cancer stage, immune microenvironment, and spatial tissue architecture using computational analyses based in artificial intelligence and machine learning. The obtained spatial phenotypes of every sample were integrated with the patients’ clinical outcomes and human papillomavirus (HPV) status.
Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing specific immune, tumor and stromal cell compositions.

very sample were integrated with the patients’ clinical outcomes and human papillomavirus (HPV) status.
Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing specific immune, tumor and stromal cell compositions. Cellular neighborhoods showed unique spatial arrangements between cell types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increase in T cell infiltration at the invasive carcinoma and formation of immune cell belts at tumor transition zones. Analysis of the expression of functional markers for antigen presentation, interferon activation, cell proliferation, and DNA damage showed particular spatial cell-to-cell interactions that accounted for functional expression patterns, such as an increased expression of oncogenic protein cMyc and a reduced expression of tumor suppressive protein p53 in Ki67+ tumor cells, as well as an increased expression of exhaustion markers, like TIM3 in CD8+ T cells. These unique spatial tissue architecture and expression profiles were also related to the HPV and BMT status of the FA patients. 
Conclusions. We identified unique spatial cellular arrangements and cell-to-cell interactions among tumor, immune and stromal cells in FA-SCC tumors, where an increase in CD8+ T cells infiltration with a rather exhausted phenotype and oncogenic expression in tumor cells was shown to correlate with the progression of the disease.
Translational and Clinical Applicability. FA patients, given their underlying DNA repair deficiency, should ideally avoid chemotherapy for treating cancer. The identification of functional immune-tumor interactions can be translated into potential actionable targets and biomarkers for immunotherapy and precision oncology that can benefit FA patients.

hemotherapy for treating cancer. The identification of functional immune-tumor interactions can be translated into potential actionable targets and biomarkers for immunotherapy and precision oncology that can benefit FA patients.

Objective: To generate and validate computational models that predict the cell fate of FA HSPCs based on the MYC-p53 regulatory motif. 
Methods: Using single cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) datasets from FA HSPCs (Rodríguez et al., Cell Stem Cell, 2021) we inferred MYC. and interactions were incorporated into a discrete Boolean network model that integrates the MYC-p53 intracellular network as well as its interactions with extracellular factors, such as the pro-inflammatory cytokines TGF and TNF.

Combinatorial mutations of the network elements in which the MYC-p53 motif is embedded will be tested using a single-cell CRISPR screening. 
Results: HJURP and RLP40 were inferred as MYC and p53 regulators from the FA HSPCs scRNAseq datasetsand incorporated into the MYC-p53 network. Simulations our Boolean network model propose that TGF signaling p53 activation causing cell dead (Figure 1A). On the other hand, survival of FA cells with unrepaired DNA damage w achieved through sustained activation of TNF prompt regulatory motif NF-B and MYC prematurely activate the WIP1 phosphatase, a negative regulator of DNA damage checkpoint proteins (Figure 1B). Modeling of the DNA damage response using ODEs recapitulated the well-known oscillatory behavior of p53 in response to DNA damage his model allowed to simulate the KO of WIP1 and its graded inhibition, which can modify the oscillatory pattern of p53, leading to its stabilization and promotion of apoptosis. Combinatorial mutations of the predicted network elements will be tested using a single-cell CRISPR screening.
Conclusion: In this work we integrated bioinformatic and tools for generating mathematical models that recapitulate FA HSPCs behaviors. Our approach allowed us to predict new components of the MYC-p53 regulatory network and simulate the behavior of FA cells under multiple .

Clinical applicability: Mathematical modeling of the FA regulatory networks has the potential to guide target inference and experimental design. We expect to actionable and new therapeutic opportunities for FA bone marrow failure.

Spatial analysis of human and murine FA tumors shows hypofunctional immune architecture related to cancer progression 
Pablo Siliceo-Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera-Mencia3, Luis A. Flores-Mejía1,2, Mahiru Trejo-León1, Fernando Pérez-Villatoro3, Anna Laury3, Renee Fisher4, Michael Epperly4, Aliesha González-Arenas1, Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6,7,8, Joel Greenberger4, Eunike Velleuer9, Anniina Färkkilä3,10,11, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2
Introduction: Clinical manifestations of Fanconi anemia (FA) include bone marrow failure and an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) at an early age. Their underlying DNA repair deficiency makes patients with FA hypersensitive to most conventional DNA-damage-based cancer treatments, stressing the need for alternative therapeutic approaches. The implementation of single-cell spatial technologies in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) have shown promising insights towards the development of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses.
Objective: Identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using single-cell spatial proteomics. 
Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis using whole-slide tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) in a total of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO.

t-CycIF) in a total of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of immunofluorescent staining (t-CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies, as well as H&E staining on sequential slides for pathologist annotation of regions of interest (ROI). The resulting cyclic scans were aligned and registered (ASHLAR) into a multidimensional image that was analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO) in the context of their tumor region, carcinogenic progression, tumor-immune architecture, and functional interactions.
Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression (Figure 1A). In human FA SCCs, there was an increase in cellular neighborhoods with DNA damage and an enrichment of tumor-associated macrophages and exhausted T cells along carcinogenic progression (Figure 1B). In the 4NQO carcinogenesis mouse model, changes in the FA mouse epithelium appeared early during treatment (8 weeks), in comparison to wild type mice, including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage (H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells (Figure 1C).

s), in comparison to wild type mice, including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage (H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells (Figure 1C). 
Conclusion: We profiled the TME of both human and mouse FA SCCs through image-based single-cell proteomics and identified unique cellular neighborhoods along carcinogenic progression, where hypofunctional immune infiltration correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocalization with immunosuppressive cell types. 
Translational and Clinical Applicability: Through this project we are discovering novel insights accounting for spatial interactions involved in cancer development and immune evasion in FA SCC. Our data has produced clear hypothesis that can further be explored and validated experimentally to develop new immunotherapeutic combinations focusing on patient stratification and improved treatment regimes. 
Figure 1. (A) Recurrent cellular neighborhoods mapped in the t-CycIF image (top) as a Voronoi plot (bottom) show an increase in neighborhood diversity with cancer progression. (B) t-CycIF image of hypopharynx tumor from a patient with FA showing the expression of gH2AX and infiltrating immune cells. (C) t-CycIF image of wild type and FA mouse model showing gH2AX, Ki67 and infiltrating immune cells a different times of treatment with 4NQO.

Introduction: Fanconi Anemia (FA) is a genetic syndrome caused by an impaired FA/BRCA DNA repair pathway. Clinical manifestations of the disease include bone marrow failure and an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) at an early age. Their underlying DNA repair deficiency makes patients with FA hypersensitive to most conventional DNA-damage-based cancer treatments, stressing the need for alternative therapeutic approaches. The implementation of single-cell spatial technologies in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) have shown promising insights towards the development of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses.
Objective: Identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using single-cell spatial proteomics. 
Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis using whole-slide tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of immunofluorescent staining (t-CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies, as well as H&E staining on sequential slides for pathologist annotation of regions of interest (ROI).

Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression (Figure 1A). Spatial analysis of FA tumors identifies conserved topological features associated with immune exhaustion niches
Topic: Cancer
Pablo Siliceo1, Cruz Osuna1, Luis Raul Figueroa Martínez2, Haydeé Peruyero2, Sonia del Marro7, Ada Junquera-Mencia3, Saundarya Shah3, 10Anna Laury3, Renee Fisher4, Michael Epperly4, 9Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6, Ramón García-Escudero7, 10Eunike Velleuer8, Anniina Färkkilä3 , Alfredo Rodríguez1
1Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México. pablosiliceo@iibiomedicas.unam.mx 2Centro de Ciencias Matemáticas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Morelia, Mexico. 3Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States of America. 5Laboratorio de Citogenética, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 6Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 7Molecular and Translational Oncology Unit, Centro de Investigaciones Energéticas, Medioambientales y Tecnológicas (CIEMAT), Avda. Complutense 40, 28040, Madrid, Spain. 8Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 9Instituto Estatal de Cancerología "Dr. Arturo Beltrán Ortega", Guerrero, México. 10Laboratory of Genome Maintenance, Rockefeller University, New York, NY 10065, USA *Corresponding author
Introduction: Fanconi anemia is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an exacerbated risk to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC).

0Laboratory of Genome Maintenance, Rockefeller University, New York, NY 10065, USA *Corresponding author
Introduction: Fanconi anemia is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an exacerbated risk to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). First-line treatments, like chemotherapy, are commonly avoided in these patients due to their inherent hypertoxicity to DNA-damaging agents, narrowing therapeutic options. The use of spatial omics in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) has paved the way for the identification of native interactions and spatial arrangements that could be therapeutically targeted. Spatial data analyses of tissues have leveraged approaches often used in eco-geographical data to decant foundational properties in uncharted landscapes. Methods like neighborhood analysis and topological data analysis, allow for the identification of compositional and structural niches, respectively, underlining complex systems like cancer. 
Objective: Identify key spatial features driving immune evasion and tumor development in the TME of FA SCC using spatial omics.
Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed immunofluorescence imaging (t-CycIF) using an immuno-oncological panel (>20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies) in a total of 57 samples from SCC. Samples were obtained from solid tumors of both non-FA and FA patients, as well as chemical (4NQO oral treatment) and spontaneous (Tp53 conditional KO) carcinogenesis models in wild-type and FA mice (Fancd2-/- and Fanca-/-, respectively). H&E of adjacent tissue cuts from each sample were used for pathological annotation (dysplasia, invasive carcinoma and adjacent stroma). Cells were segmented (StarDist) and each marker quantified (MCMICRO) as the median marker intensity within each cell.

of adjacent tissue cuts from each sample were used for pathological annotation (dysplasia, invasive carcinoma and adjacent stroma). Cells were segmented (StarDist) and each marker quantified (MCMICRO) as the median marker intensity within each cell. Finally, data analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods to identify spatial features related to immune suppression and cancer progression. Cellular neighborhood identification (SCIMAP) was used to identify repeating cellular niches, while topological data analysis (), a method grounded in the progressive generation of geometrical networks in space, was used to identify repeating geometrical motifs between relevant cell types
Results: We found phenotypical heterogeneity between sample groups (FA and non-FA) and annotated tumor regions (dysplasia and invasive carcinoma), highlighting a striking recruitment of myeloid cells at the adjacent stroma of tumor regions with increased damage (H2AX) and proliferation (Ki67) signals in FA samples (both in human and murine samples). Neighborhood analysis identified cellular niches of dendritic cells, Tregs and M2 macrophages co-occurring with exhausted (TIM3+ and PD-1+) Tcells in FA samples, showing differential interactions in the stroma and epithelium. On the other hand, found global topological footprints differentiating between early dysplasia and invasive carcinoma, while also detecting fine-grained neighborhood-specific geometrical features associated with previously found myeloid-mediated Tcell exhaustion.
Conclusion: We have characterized through spatial proteomics the TME of human and murine FA SCCs, as well as non-FA SCCs, and identified unique FA-specific topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression.

ell exhaustion.
Conclusion: We have characterized through spatial proteomics the TME of human and murine FA SCCs, as well as non-FA SCCs, and identified unique FA-specific topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression. 
translationalThis project provides a hypothesis-generating of the TME of FA SCCs, focusing on patient stratification and the exploration of novel immunotherapeutic approaches to treat patients.

1.Introducción La anemia de Fanconi (FA) es un síndrome de inestabilidad genómica causado por alteraciones genéticas en la vía de reparación de ADN, FA/BRCA . Dentro 
del fenotipo clínico destaca una incrementada predisposición a desarrollar carcinomas de células escamosas (CCE) durante el inicio de la adultez (Fig. 1A y 1B). Al tener una

r alteraciones genéticas en la vía de reparación de ADN, FA/BRCA . Dentro 
del fenotipo clínico destaca una incrementada predisposición a desarrollar carcinomas de células escamosas (CCE) durante el inicio de la adultez (Fig. 1A y 1B). Al tener una 
deficiencia en la reparación del ADN, los pacientes son hipersensibles a tratamientos clastogénicos como la quimioterapia . Nuevas tecnologías en el estudio del cáncer, como
laproteómica espacial unicelular, permiten eldesarrollo deterapias alternativas basadas enelestudio deinteracciones funcionales dentro delmicroambiente tumoral (TAM) .
2. Objetivo Estudiar la arquitectura espacial del 
MAT de CCE de pacientes con AF con el fin de 
identificar arreglos espaciales e interacciones 
funcionales con valor inmunoterapéutico .Contacto:
psiliceop@gmail.com
3. Metodología Realizamos un análisis espacial multiplex utilizando inmunofluorescencia cíclica múltiple entejido (t-CycIF) 
(Fig. 2A) en 33 cortes de tejido en laminillas obtenidos a partir de bloques de tumores embebidos en formalina y fijados en 
parafina (FFPE) demuestras deCCE depacientes con ysinAF(Fig.1B),yderatones AF(Fancd 2-/-)ysilvestres tratados
oralmente durante 20semanas con elpotente carcinogénico 4NQO (Fig. 1C). Implementamos multiples ciclos de tinción 
inmunofluorescente (t-CycIF) con un panel de >20 anticuerpos acoplados a fluorocromos (Tabla 1), fotografiado (Rarecyte 
Finder II) e inactivación de fluoróforos, además de tinciones de H&E en cortes adjacentes para anotar regiones de la 
progression (Fig. 2B). Las imagenes resultantes de cada ciclo fueron computacionalmente unidas en una imagen 
multidimensional por muestra (Fig. 2C) en donde se realizó una segmentación del área de cada célula en la imagen basado en

regiones de la 
progression (Fig. 2B). Las imagenes resultantes de cada ciclo fueron computacionalmente unidas en una imagen 
multidimensional por muestra (Fig. 2C) en donde se realizó una segmentación del área de cada célula en la imagen basado en 
la señal de la tinción del ADN (Hoechst) (Fig. 2D). Cada célula fue clasificada con base en su perfil de expresión de acuerdo a su 
identidad (inmune , tumoral o stromal) y su fenotipo functional (Fig. 2E). Finalmente, se hizo una análisis espacial para la 
identificación de vecindarios celulares recurrentes (RCN) a partir de la composición espacial alrededor de cada célula (Fig. 2F). 
Los RCN luego fueron graficados como polígonos en la imagen para su visualización (Fig. 2G).
